title: ⚠ MAP KEY — these are MODERN political borders
description: All coloured regions are MODERN country borders. They do NOT represent pre-Columbian civilizations (Olmec, Maya, Aztec), the Spanish colonial empire, or early African colonial territories.

Mexico (modern borders) — Aztec heartland
description: Modern political border (post-1910 Revolution). ORIGIN of cacao as cultural and economic institution — Tenochtitlan (now Mexico City), Aztec currency system, xocolatl ritual drink. Still a minor producer today.

title: Ecuador (modern borders) — ancestral origin of cacao
description: Modern political border. DNA evidence (Zarrillo et al., 2018) places the earliest domesticated cacao in the upper Amazon basin — southeastern Ecuador ~5,300 years ago. Nacional variety is the living heirloom.

Côte d'Ivoire (modern borders) — largest producer today
description: Modern political border (post-1960 independence). 42% of world cacao. The industry is built on child labour despite legal frameworks and 'fair trade' certification schemes — documented ongoing violations (US Dept of Labor Child Labor Reports 2020).

Ghana (modern borders) — 17% of world production
description: Modern political border (post-1957 independence — first sub-Saharan African colony to gain independence). British introduced cacao 1879; now 17% of world supply.

title: Nigeria + Cameroon (modern borders) — 7% + emerging production
description: Modern political borders. Combined ~10% of world cacao.

title: Indonesia (modern borders) — 13% of world production
description: Modern political border. Sulawesi is the main cacao island. 13% of world cacao.

title: Brazil / Bahia (modern borders) — historical major producer
description: Modern political border. Bahia state anchored Brazilian cacao through the 19th–20th centuries; 1989 witch-broom fungus devastated the industry. Slow ongoing recovery.
